title: Can You Reuse Landscape Fabric? Lifespan, Tips, and What to Know
author: Marcus Halvorsen

seo.metaDescription: Can you reuse landscape fabric? Learn when reuse is smart, how long fabric lasts, and tips to extend its life for a weed-free, budget-friendly garden.

social.excerpt: Can you reuse landscape fabric? Yes, if you know what to look for. DOTDAY covers fabric lifespan, the signs it is time to replace, and tips to extend its life. #DOTDAY #landscapefabric #weedbarrier #gardentips #sustainablegardening

hero.excerpt: As seasons shift and garden designs change, a common question comes up: can you reuse landscape fabric without compromising its effectiveness? Here is the honest answer, plus how to tell when to replace it.

image.alt: Stretching a DOTDAY woven fabric to check whether you can reuse landscape fabric
image.caption: A durable woven fabric, lifted carefully and cleaned, can often be reused in a new project.

quickAnswer.heading: The short answer
items: You can often reuse landscape fabric if it lifts out intact, without major tears
Woven polypropylene lasts longest and reuses best; biodegradable fabric does not
Replace it if you see multiple tears, gray brittle discoloration, or heavy clogging
Lift carefully, clean off soil and roots, and store dry to extend its life
Starting fresh? A quality woven fabric like SHIELD or XBAR reuses for years

blocks.heading: Can you reuse landscape fabric?
blocks.body: Many DIY gardeners depend on landscape fabric as a weed barrier, but as garden designs change a common question comes up: can you reuse landscape fabric without losing its effectiveness? Yes, you often can, and it largely depends on its condition. First, evaluate the fabric. If it is intact without significant tears, it can usually be repurposed. Look for wear indicators like holes, frays, or discoloration. Fabric breaks down mainly through UV exposure, so pieces left uncovered or in harsh sun wear out faster. Proper removal matters: lift the fabric carefully without tearing it and clean off dirt and plant material before reuse. Planning a fresh install instead? Size it with the [DOTDAY Fabric Calculator](https://www.thedotday.com/landscape-fabric-calculator).

blocks.heading: Average lifespan by fabric type
rows: Reusable if intact and not sun-damaged
Designed to break down, not for reuse

blocks.heading: When to replace instead of reuse
blocks.body: Reusing fabric saves money and resources, but sometimes replacement is the right call. Replace it if you see damage in the form of multiple tears or holes, discoloration where the fabric has gone grayish or brittle from UV breakdown, or heavy soil accumulation and root growth that clog the weave and stop water passing through. If the wear is minor, the fabric is likely still functional and a simple clean and repair will get you another season or two.

blocks.alt: Planting beds before and after a DOTDAY woven fabric, the kind of durable fabric you can reuse
blocks.caption: Woven fabric in good condition holds its structure, which is what makes reuse practical.

blocks.heading: Re-fabricating multiple sites this season?
blocks.body: DOTDAY supplies woven fabric to landscapers and growers direct. Request bulk and pro pricing for fresh coverage across jobs.

blocks.heading: Tips to extend the life of your fabric

steps.title: Store it dry
steps.body: After removing fabric from the garden, keep it in a cool, dry place. Avoid damp spots that lead to mold.

steps.title: Clean before reuse
steps.body: Gently hose down the fabric to remove dirt and debris so the barrier stays effective in its next spot.

steps.title: Reinstall with proper overlap
steps.body: When you lay it again, secure it well and overlap seams so weeds cannot grow through the gaps.

steps.title: Keep it covered
steps.body: Cover with mulch, gravel, or rock. Sun exposure is the main reason fabric becomes brittle and unusable.

blocks.heading: Reuse pays off most with quality fabric
blocks.body: The fabric most worth reusing is woven and UV-stabilized to begin with. [SHIELD](https://www.thedotday.com/product-page/dotday-shield-weed-barrier-fabric-heavy-duty-3-2oz-landscape-fabric) 3.2oz handles beds and mulch, [XBAR](https://www.thedotday.com/product-page/dotday-xbar-weed-barrier-fabric-heavy-duty-5oz-dual-layer) 5oz handles gravel and traffic, and [TERRA](https://www.thedotday.com/product-page/dotday-terra-geotextile-fabric-4-oz-non-woven-landscape-fabric) is built for drainage. Higher-quality fabric simply survives more lift-and-relay cycles. To compare durability, read [what landscape fabric lasts the longest](https://www.thedotday.com/post/what-landscape-fabric-lasts-the-longest).

blocks.heading: Find the right fabric in two minutes
blocks.body: Answer a few questions about your ground condition and get matched to SHIELD, XBAR, or TERRA for the job.

items.a: Yes, you can often reuse landscape fabric if it lifts out intact without significant tears, holes, or brittle discoloration. Woven polypropylene fabric reuses best because it holds its structure. Lift it carefully, clean off soil and roots, store it dry, and reinstall with proper seam overlap. Biodegradable fabric is the exception, since it is designed to break down.

items.q: How long does landscape fabric last?
items.a: It depends on the material. Light polypropylene weed barrier typically lasts 5 to 10 years, while woven landscape fabric can last 10 to 20 years, making it the better long-term and reusable choice. Biodegradable fabric lasts only 1 to 3 years by design. Keeping fabric covered from sunlight is the single biggest factor in reaching the top of those ranges.

items.q: When should you replace landscape fabric instead of reusing it?
items.a: Replace it when you see multiple tears or holes, a gray and brittle appearance from UV breakdown, or heavy soil and root clogging that stops water passing through. Those signal the fabric can no longer suppress weeds or drain properly. Minor wear is fine to reuse after a clean, but structural damage means it is time for a fresh piece.
